coredata.dc:title: Seed germination studies on Chickpeas, Barley, Mung beans and Wheat with natural biomass and plastic waste derived C-dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chauhan, Pooja
Kumari, Manisha
Chaudhary, Savita
Chaudhary, Ganga Ram
Umar, Ahmad
Baskoutas, Sotirios
coredata.dc:description: Agronomical providence of nanoparticles in enhancing food productivity has brought new revolution in agricultural sector. However, the comprehensive ingenuity of their synergetic impact on environment and living flora and fauna is still poorly explored. The current study endeavours to tackle this apprehension by systematically exploring the agronomical paradigm of six different types of C-dots derived from natural biomass and plastic waste on the four different types of seeds viz. black chick peas (Cicer arietinum), barley (Hordeum vulgare), mung beans (Vigna radiata) and wheat (Triticum aestivum) at room temperature. C-dots have displayed a dose responsive effect (250 to 5000 mg/L) on the growth of chosen seeds, including the elongation of root length and coleoptile length. The development of seedlings under atmospheric conditions exhibited excellent physiological stability in presence of synthesized C-dots for all types of seeds with concentrations as high as 3000 mg/L for dry seed. The direct exposure of C-dots resulted in enhanced growth as compared to the water exposure and considered as the most important novel aspect of present work. The developed C-dots provide more nutrient content and easy penetration to the seeds due to their enhanced surface area and very small size. The germination and Vigor index have also been augmented in presence of C-dots after 7 days of exposure. C-dots have affected the chlorophyll content in mung beans as a function of time and concentration. The developed C-dots possess excellent biocompatible behaviour and help in the complete growth of the different types of seeds which suggest their enhanced utilization in the agronomical field. This is one of the detailed studies, which explore the impact of C-dots on widely used food crops with the non-toxic and biocompatible C-dots. The information achieved herein will allow the usage of C-dots as a capable nanopriming agent for the natural germination of seeds.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Agronomical providence of nanoparticles in enhancing food productivity has brought new revolution in agricultural sector. However, the comprehensive ingenuity of their synergetic impact on environment and living flora and fauna is still poorly explored. The current study endeavours to tackle this apprehension by systematically exploring the agronomical paradigm of six different types of C-dots derived from natural biomass and plastic waste on the four different types of seeds viz. black chick peas (Cicer arietinum), barley (Hordeum vulgare), mung beans (Vigna radiata) and wheat (Triticum aestivum) at room temperature. C-dots have displayed a dose responsive effect (250 to 5000 mg/L) on the growth of chosen seeds, including the elongation of root length and coleoptile length. The development of seedlings under atmospheric conditions exhibited excellent physiological stability in presence of synthesized C-dots for all types of seeds with concentrations as high as 3000 mg/L for dry seed. The direct exposure of C-dots resulted in enhanced growth as compared to the water exposure and considered as the most important novel aspect of present work. The developed C-dots provide more nutrient content and easy penetration to the seeds due to their enhanced surface area and very small size. The germination and Vigor index have also been augmented in presence of C-dots after 7 days of exposure. C-dots have affected the chlorophyll content in mung beans as a function of time and concentration. The developed C-dots possess excellent biocompatible behaviour and help in the complete growth of the different types of seeds which suggest their enhanced utilization in the agronomical field. This is one of the detailed studies, which explore the impact of C-dots on widely used food crops with the non-toxic and biocompatible C-dots. The information achieved herein will allow the usage of C-dots as a capable nanopriming agent for the natural germination of seeds.

ce:section.ce:para: The feeding demands of growing population have pushed the agricultural sector to search innovative ideas for enhancing crop productivity (Rivera-Padilla, 2020; Huang et al., 2021a, 2021b). Out of various factors, the germination of seed is one of the most crucial stages in life cycle of plants for better productivity of any kind of crop (Aliyar et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021a, 2021b; Shamya Arokia Rajan et al., 2020). Mainly, seed germination begins by initiating the physiochemical processes within the seed after absorption of water (Eker and Karakaya, 2020; Chaudhary et al., 2018a, 2018b, 2018c). However, oxidative damage and storage under air dry conditions have caused the significant decline in the germination ability of seed and lowered the crop productivity (Adetunji et al., 2021; Kamal Kumar et al., 2020; Rifna et al., 2019; Alouane et al., 2021). Till date, various methods like hardening, priming and coating of seeds are used to improve the seedling growth (Blunk et al., 2019; Yamauchi and Chuong, 1995). However, the utilization of pesticides, bactericides, plant growth regulators and micro fertilizers in seed protection has possessed serious side effects on the environment and living flora and fauna (Hamed et al., 2018; Mikula et al., 2020; Rutkowska et al., 2020; Stamford et al., 2019). Therefore, it is necessary to develop alternative cost-effective methodologies for paving the way for sustainable approach to improve seed germination abilities of crops.

In this regard, nanotechnology has emerged as one of the effective way with high potential impact in agriculture sector (Rohilla et al., 2020; Takeuchi et al., 2014). The application of nanotechnology has transformed the agriculture at molecular level and act as good growth promoter for different types of crops (Usman et al., 2020). The nanotechnology is considered as one of the leading ways to develop low-cost materials to improve the cultivation and growth productivity rate of plants (Marella et al., 2021). Till date, different types of nanoparticles such as iron oxide, zinc oxide, cadmium selenide, selenium, titanium oxide, nano copper have been widely used in different types of agronomic practices (Chaudhary et al., 2018a; Itroutwar et al., 2020; Tovar et al., 2020). Carbon dots (C-dots) with outstanding optical and fluorescent properties with higher biocompatibility and low toxicity have possessed significant potential in agricultural sector (Chauhan et al., 2020a, 2020b). C-dots have an enormous potential to enhance the agricultural production by managing the toxicity impact of nanoparticles. The small size and amendable surface functionalization have further enhanced their ability to easily enter the plant cells and interact with intracellular structures of plants and affect their growth behaviour (Chauhan et al., 2021a; Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020; Mazlan et al., 2019). In addition, the high surface area of C-dots makes them effect contender for delivering herbicides, agrochemicals or genes (Nagarajan et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2021). In literature both types of detrimental and beneficial effects of C-dots on agronomic traits, yield and productivity of plants including modification in the nutritional value of food crops have been documented (Lakshmi et al., 2020; Meena et al., 2019). For instance, the germination rate and seedling growth of various plants are inhibited in presence of nanomaterials (Mu and Han, 2022). It has been found that the excessive production of reactive oxygen species and blockage of nutrient transport channels due to aggregation of C-dots are mainly responsible for the reduction in growth productivity. On the other hand, the Carbon-based nanomaterials are found to be quite effective fertilizers and empowering agricultural science through a wide range of advancements in crop yield (Bajpai et al., 2019; Fang et al., 2021; Chauhan et al., 2021b; Chen et al., 2020; Muhammad et al., 2021; Yalikun et al., 2021; Won and Kim, 2021; Wang et al., 2020). Further, in today's world it is very difficult and challenging to decompose plastic and bio-waste. So, the basic theme of present work involves utilization of waste material as a precursor for the development of C-dots. In literature, various techniques and methodologies have been reported for fabricating C-dots. However, there corresponding effect on the seed growth is limited. The current work offered an attempt to tackle the synergetic impact of C-dots on seed germination ability of four different types of seeds viz. Black Chickpeas, Barley, Mung beans and Wheat at room temperature. C-dots with size range of 2–10 nm obtained from six different sources of natural biomass and plastic waste has been chosen to investigate the agronomical paradigm. The different analytical studies taken into consideration to explore the characteristic absorption, emission and structural behaviour of developed C-dots. Afterwards, the influential effect of developed C-dots has been investigated on the germination activity of different types of seeds. The dose dependent responsive effect on the growth, root length and coleoptile length of seeds has also been investigated out in a deeper manner. The presence of high concentration amount of C-dots has enhanced the physiological stability of all chosen seedling (Chauhan et al., 2021a, 2020a, 2020b). The statistical analysis of C-dots on the toxicity assessment of plants especially on seeds is not yet explored in extensive manner in literature. This current work offers a detailed study on exploring the impact of C-dots on widely used food crops. Additionally, the works deals with the evaluation of the toxicity and quantify the attained data to find out the plausible threats and breaches about the safe use of C-dots in agricultural fields. In addition, the easy processing strategy has offered an outstanding scope of C-dots in industrial sector. The different kinds of contaminants especially toxic metal ions and harmful organic dyes released from the industries can be easily sensed in selective and sensitive manner via using C-dots.

The seeds of black chick peas, barley, mung beans and wheat were purchased from local market of Sector 41, Chandigarh, India. The waste agarose gel was obtained from Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh. The waste plastic bottles, cups and polybags were acquired from the trash can of Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh. The waste coconut coir and Pearl millet seeds were procured from local market of Serctor-15 Chandigarh. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and ethanol with 99% purity were acquired from Fisher Scientific India. Quinine sulphate dye with 99% purity was procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized water was utilized as a dispersing solvent to prepare the sample solutions. All the chemical reagents were used as such without doing any further purification.

The facile thermal calcination approach has been used for the fabrication of highly fluorescent C-dots from the plastic waste such as bottles, cups and polybags as precursor source. To describe briefly, the known amount of the plastic waste has been thermally calcined under nitrogen atmosphere in a muffle furnace for 3 h at 400 °C. Afterwards, the obtained powdered has been cooled down to room temperature and mechanically crushed and grinded to form fine powder. 1 g of each obtained powder from waste plastic of bottles, cups and polybags has further been dispersed in 200 mL of deionized water and kept under stirring for 24 h. The plastic waste such as bottles, cups and polybags made up of polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polypropylene and polyethylene respectively as a precursor source. The obtained solutions have further been centrifuged at 7500 rpm for 15 min. (Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020). The obtained supernatants of the respective samples containing C-dots have further purified by filtering through 0.22 μm filter membrane. The dialysis was carried out to obtain CQD1 (polybags), CQD2 (cups) and CQD3 (bottles). The obtained C-dots have further lyophilized to remove the moisture content and stored at room temperature for further use in analysis (Fig. 1 ). The comparison of present work with wet chemistry along with challenges and industrial applications has been represented in supporting file.

C-dots composed of biomass-based carbon sources which consist of bio-waste such as agar gel, coconut coir and pearl millet were synthesized via employing one-step hydro-thermal calcination treatment. In the typical procedure, fine powder of coconut coir, pearl millet and biohazard waste containing agar gel were added into silica crucibles and transferred into muffle furnace at a temperature of 300 °C for 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The as obtained powders in silica crucibles were gathered from the furnace and cooled off to room temperature. The resultant carbonized powders were grounded well to make fine powders of carbon. After that, 0.5 g of formed powders was sprinkled into 100 mL of deionized water and subjected to stirring for 20 h. Subsequently, the carbonized solutions were collected and ultracentrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm to remove the solid residues. The supernatants of the resultant solutions were further filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane along with dialysis scheme and as-obtained CQD4 (agar gel), CQD4 (coconut coir) and CQD6 (pearl millet) were reserved for the further work (Chauhan et al., 2021a; Chauhan et al., 2020a, 2020b).

The absorption spectrum of developed C-dots was obtained from LABINDIA UV–vis. spectrophotometer having wavelength region of 200–800 nm with quartz cuvette of fixed path length of 1 cm. The fluorescence spectrum of prepared C-dots has been acquired using Perkin Elmer LS 55 PL spectrophotometer. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was carried out on Malvern (ZEN 1690) to investigate the zeta potential and size distribution profile of C-dots. The crystalline properties of C-dots have been studied using XRD analysis with a scan rate of 2 deg min−1 in Panalytical X'Pert Pro diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. The morphological structure of C-dots was determined using a High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopic (HRTEM, Hitachi H7500 electron microscope). The existence of different types of functional groups over the surface of C-dots have been analyzed on PerkinElmer (RX1) FTIR spectrometer instrument in the KBr medium. Mettler Toledo digital pH meter have been used to access the acidic and basic behaviour of C-dots obtained from natural bio and plastic waste. REMI R-24 centrifuge have been used to carrying out the ultracentrifugation of prepared C-dots solutions. AICIL muffle furnace has been applied for carrying out the thermal calcinations of the precursor materials. All the measurements were recorded at room temperature.

The respective values of quantum yield (Φ) have been calculated to visualize the absorption and emissive behaviour of prepared C-dots. The respective solution of Quinine sulphate dye in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution has been utilized as a standard reference material during the analysis by using the following equation (Chauhan et al., 2020a, 2020b).
(1)

where φ refers to the quantum yield values, C subscript corresponds to prepared C-dots sample, R subscript referred to the reference (Quinine sulphate dye), I refers to the integrated emission intensity, ɳ corresponds to the refraction index of system (here ɳ = 1, as aqueous system has been used), and A refers to the absorbance values obtained from the UV–vis. absorption spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of seedlings and their germination studies
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, all the chosen seeds have been sterilized with mercuric chloride (HgCl2) solution for 5 min to remove all types of bacterial contaminations (Chaudhary et al., 2018b, 2019a). The excessive residual amount of HgCl2 from seeds has been washed with deionized water for 10–15 min. Two set measurements have been applied to investigate the germinating ability of chosen seeds. For first kind of measurements, dry seeds were washed with mercury chloride followed by water and then placed directly in contact of different concentration solutions of C-dots. In second case, dry seeds were initially dipped in distilled water solution for 24 h and allowed to sprout. Afterwards, sprouted seeds were shifted to petri-plates containing different concentration solution of C-dots (250, 500, 1000, 3000 and 5000 mg/L). The choice of concentration has been based on our preliminary experiment. Equal quantity of 30 seeds has been placed in different petri plates containing 5 mL of aqueous solution of nanoparticles for maintaining the equivalency in measurements (Tombuloglu et al., 2019a). The seeds soaked in deionized water have been chosen as control. All the experiments were performed thrice to evaluate the repeatability of system. The grown roots of the seeds were measured with the help of thread. The measurements of different pairs of seminal roots, leaves, coleoptile and radicals emerged out from the germinated seeds after incubation period of 3 and 7 days have been chosen for the analysis (Chaudhary et al., 2018b, 2019a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of different parameters using seed germination assay
ce:section.ce:para:
Different parameters viz. germination ability, germination index, percentage inhabitation growth, Vigor index I and seed Vigor II been calculated by using the following equations for Chickpeas, Barley, Mung beans and Wheat (Al-Amri et al., 2020).

where St and Sc correspond to the total number of germinated seeds in case of C-dots and control sample and Lt and Lc correspond to the length of germinated seeds in case of C-dots and control. Cc and Cd refers to the average root length of seeds present in control and C-dots sample. All the measurements have been carried out when the length of root and coleoptile reached to the maximum after 7 days respectively (Chaudhary et al., 2019b; Tombuloglu et al., 2019b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of chlorophyll content in leaves of Mung beans seeds
ce:section.ce:para:
The chlorophyll content value has further been calculated as a function of different concentration of C-dots in the leaves of sprouted Mung beans in deionized water system. However, the absence of leaves in the sprouted seeds of Chickpeas, Barley and Wheat has restricted us to analyse chlorophyll content in these seeds. Initially, the fully-fledged green leaves of sprouted Mung beans have been kept in 95% ethanol for partial hydration (Tombuloglu et al., 2019c, 2019d). Afterwards, fine paste of leaves was prepared and again soaked in 85% ethanol for complete lysis of green leaves of sprouted Mung beans. Subsequently, the obtained paste was allowed to stand in dark for 45 min and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The obtained supernatant of respective samples has been collected and their UV–vis. absorption spectrum has been recorded at 645 and 665 nm wavelength (Chauhan et al., 2020a, 2020b). Chlorophyll content was measured using the following formulas (Tombuloglu et al., 2020a):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of moisture content in leaves of Mung beans seeds
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of water uptake or moisture content of Mung beans seeds has been determined in presence of different C-dots by using Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Before analysis, Mung beans seeds have been allowed to incubate in the presence of different concentration of C-dots and deionized water (control) at room temperature for 4 h. Moisture level of Mung beans seeds was obtained by calculating the mass loss of Mung beans seeds when heated in range of 25 to 250 °C with a constant rate of 10 °C/min. The relative mass loss values will provide the sufficient information about the moisture loss. In addition, the dry seeds and water absorbed seeds of Mung beans have been taken as reference. The dry, treated and untreated seeds were evaluated using the same steps, respectively. The measurements have been carried out in triplicates to check the reproducibility of system (Tombuloglu et al., 2020b; Xin et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and functional analysis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The nature of the surface of developed C-dots has been assessed by using the FTIR analysis (Fig. 2a). In case of CQD1, the major peaks at 3450, 2911, 2841, 1628, 1466, 1337, 1057 and 722 cm−1 has been observed due to the stretching and bending of OH, CH, CO and COC and CC alkene over the surface of CQD1 (Table S1). However, the major peaks at 3580, 1558, 1408, 1019 and 912 cm−1 has been observed in case of CQD2. CQDs derived from plastic bottle (CQD3) have displayed the major peaks at 3660, 2345, 2111, 1718, 1589, 1395 and 985 cm−1 respectively. Whereas, CQDs derived from agar gel have displayed major peaks at 3320, 2350 and 1560, 1390 and 1096, 885 and 673 cm−1. These peaks have been mainly aroused due to the OH stretch, CH and CO stretching, CC, CO stretching vibration, NH bending and CH stretching mode in CQD4 (Yagmur et al., 2020). In addition, the C-dots derived from coconut coir has displayed major peaks at 2960, 1450, 1106, 870 and 650 cm−1 (Chauhan et al., 2021a; Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020; Tang et al., 2020). These peaks are aroused due to existence of CH stretching, CH2 bending, CO stretching, CH and NH bending and aromatic sp2 CH bending over the surface of CQD5. However, the CQDs derived from pearl millets (CQD6) have displayed the major peaks at 3191, 2756, 2262, 2120, 1417 and 1191 cm−1. These peaks are mainly associated with the existence of CH stretching, OH stretching of carboxylic acid group, CN group, CC stretching, CH bending and CO bond stretching over the surface of formed particles (Zhang et al., 2018). From here it can be concluded that surface of developed CQDs has been completely adhered by the different functional groups and reflects their complete formation and stable nature with less chances of agglomeration (Chauhan et al., 2021a; Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020). The hydrodynamic diameter of prepared CQDs have been found in range 4–50 nm along with their PDI value varied between 0.3 and 0.5 (Table S2). The existence of lower value of polydispersity index has suggested the higher stability and lesser chances of agglomeration in homogenous media. The morphological analysis using HRTEM studies and their size distribution have suggested the formation of particles in size range of 2–10 nm (Table S2 and Figs. S1, S2). The crystalline and amorphous nature of prepared CQDs has further been assessed by using XRD analysis (Fig. 2b). The sharpening of peaks in XRD pattern suggests the crystalline nature of C-dots. All the formed particles have been found to be crystalline in nature with size varies between 5 and 30 nm (Table S2). In addition, the sharpening of peaks in XRD pattern has further supported the crystalline nature of prepared CQDs (Zhao et al., 2020).

The number of possible defective sites and number of unit cells present on the exterior core of developed CQDs have further been calculated from the dislocation density values from XRD data. For instance, the value of dislocation density has been found to be 48.22, 36.95, 94.25, 625, 64 and 42.22 (10−13) m−2 for CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, CQD4, CQD5 and CQD6 respectively. On interpreting the data, it has been found that the decreased value of dislocation density on varying the precursor source has mainly been explained due to the decreased number of unit cell and higher crystalline growth of particles. Therefore, by varying the nature of precursor source, the crystallinity of particles can be easily controlled (Chauhan et al., 2021a; Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterization of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The aim and central theme of current work is to use the biocongenial C-dots obtained from naturally available biomass and plastic waste for analysing the effect on the germination rate of four commonly used seeds. The exploitation of plastic waste and biomass has acted as a potential precursor for the development of highly economic, fluorescent, and biocompatible C-dots. Thus, the current work reflects the strategy for the useful conversion of biodegradable and non-biodegradable material into highly rich source of carbon via thermal calcination at different temperature (Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020; Lv et al., 2019). The respective effect of chosen precursor source on the optical properties of CQDs has been assessed by carrying out the optical and emission spectroscopic studies as a function of precursor source (Fig. 3 ). All the prepared CQDs have displayed the absorption peak in the of region 200–800 nm with fixed cell length of 1.0 cm (Fig. 3a). The corresponding absorption band has been majorly associated with the π-π⁎ and n-π⁎ transitions present in the different functional groups (carbonyl and hydroxyl) over the surface of prepared CQDs (Nie et al., 2014; Chauhan et al., 2020a, 2020b). However, the CQDs developed from plastic waste have shown the noticeable peak between 270 and 300 nm. On the other hand, the biowaste derived CQDs have shown the bathochromic shift in the absorption peak with evident peak at 280–350 nm. The prepared CQDs have also displayed the emission changes as a function of excitation wavelength in the range of 250–350 nm (Fig. 3b). The maximum excitation has been observed at 310 and 320 nm for plastic derived CDQs. However, the biowaste derived CQDs have displayed excitation at lower wavelength (290, 280 and 250 nm). This kind of variation was mainly explained due to the existence of different type of functional moieties present over the exterior core of C-dots (Fig. 2a).

In addition, smaller size and non-agglomerating nature has also contributed towards the optical and emission properties of CQDs prepared via using different types of precursor sources (Chauhan et al., 2020a, 2020b). The fluorescence emission behaviour of C-dots has also been visualized by calculating out their quantum yield values (Fig. S3). All the developed CQDs have exhibited more than 60% of quantum yield. From here, it can be inferred that developed CQDs have possessed outstanding emissive behaviour and find potential scope in chemo-sensing applications. In addition, their pH dependent emissive behaviour has also been explored by using fluorescence spectroscopy in range 200–800 nm with pH values varied between 2 and 12. For instance, the CQD1 have possessed higher stability at pH 6. On varying the precursor source to cups and bottle have changed the pH stability in range of 6–8 respectively. However, the biomass derived CQDs have been found to be more stable in acidic region (Pandey et al., 2020). All these variations have been explained due to the existence of different types of functional group over the surface of CQDs (Chauhan et al., 2020a, 2020b). From the obtained results, it can be clearly demonstrated that developed C-dots possess crystalline structure with average size range 2–10 nm along with excellent absorption and emission properties. Additionally, the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups along with different CH groups on the surface of C-dots suggests more idea about their structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Seed germination activity in presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed and elaborative study showing the effect of prepared C-dots on the seed germination activity of Chickpeas, Barley, Mung beans and Wheat has given an insight to the phytotoxicity and stability of developed C-dots in agricultural field. Mung beans seeds have high propensity to germination in all environmental condition and provide bulk of information about less sensitive flora species. It is assumed that the cell wall and exterior surface of Wheat seeds is very thin and highly reactive towards various types of toxicants (Barron et al., 2005; Philippe et al., 2006). Chickpeas and Barley seeds have very thick cell wall. The utilized model seeds will provide valuable information about developed C-dots towards series of plants which have thin, moderate and thick cell wall. The toxicity level of particles at different concentration level can be evaluated by measuring different parameters associated with seed germination activity. The small size of prepared C-dots has made them highly capable material to penetrate the cell walls of chosen seeds and affect their biological cycles involving effect on root length and germination activities. In addition, this kind of study has provided the detailed information about the anti-proliferative and cytotoxic effects of C-dots on chosen seeds. Subsequently, the existence of nuclei in the plants seed has given a better alternative for analysing the effect of C-dots in comparison with animal and human cells (Chaudhary et al., 2018b, 2019a).

The edible food plants play fundamental role in the ecosystem and act as a useful component of food chain on the earth-crust. Therefore, in the current study we have chosen four mainly used edible seeds i.e. Chickpeas, Barley, Mung beans and Wheat for investigating the effect and greater depth profiling of CQDs prepared from biomass and plastic waste. It is mandatory to evaluate the biological compensation of developed CQDs before utilizing them in environmental remediation process. The detailed investigation has provided an insight to the phytotoxicity and stability of developed CQDs in agricultural field. The utilized model seeds will provide valuable information about developed CQDs towards series of plants which have thin, moderate and thick cell wall as a function of concentration ranging from 250 to 5000 mg/L. In addition, the germination ability of seeds related to the first stage of plant's cycle and possessed direct influence of the external environmental stress. Therefore, mature germination index of seedlings in presence of CQDs has given a direct-evidence for explaining the augmented productivity of plants.

Fig. 4 reveals that all the taken seeds of Chickpeas, Barley, Mung beans and Wheat has shown 100% germination rate in presence of increasing amount of CQD1. Similarly, the germination of all the four types of chosen seeds were found to be 100% in presence of remaining types of taken particles i.e. CQD2 to CQD6. In case of Chickpeas and Barley, long germinated roots were observed along with small and thin seminal roots after 7 days of exposure. For Mung bean seeds, origination of one and two healthy leaves was observed whereas in case of wheat, 3 and 5-seminal roots were observed along with different plasmolyzed seeds. It was further observed that prepared CQDs have the ability to interfere in the plant development especially in metabolism process. It depends upon size, concentration and physicochemical properties of particles and causes noticeable influences impact on the germination rate (Tombuloglu et al., 2018, 2019a, 2019b, 2019c, 2019d). The nature of chosen CQDs has been explained on the basis of surface functionalization, size and shape of particles. The surface characteristics and surface area of chosen CQDs has also played a vital role in the phytotoxic activity of germinated seeds. In addition, the presence of carboxyl group over the surface of CQDs has further promoted the nuclei growth in plants.

The biocompatible nature of prepared CQDs with small size and smooth morphology have further imparted non-damaging impact over cell wall and cell organelles of chosen seeds. The nature of seed has also played a significant role in germination ability of seeds in presence of CQDs. For instance, the water absorption capacity of mature dried seeds is comparatively more for stimulating the cellular metabolism and growth rate. In addition, the rootlet emerging behaviour in seeds is also based on the type of micronuclei present in the chosen seeds (Fig. 4e). Different parameters such as average root length, coleoptile length, germination %, germination index, percentage inhibition growth, Vigor index I and Seed Vigor II have been calculated during seed germination activity to investigate influential behaviour of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of average root length and coleoptile length in presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The germination ability of chosen seeds has further been investigated by measuring the root length of seeds in both dry and sprouted seeds (Fig. 5 ). The emerging behaviour of rootlets based upon the micronuclei present in the seed which in turn depends upon the non-toxic carboxyl moieties around the surface of prepared CQDs. On moving from 3 days to 7 days exposure, the root length of all kind of seeds shows linear enhancement up to 3000 mg/L concentration level of CQDs. Both dry and sprouted seeds have excellent values as compared to the control system whereas the value of average root length of dry seeds found higher as compared to the sprouted system. It is considered as the most important outcome of the respective study. In addition, the maximum root length of Chickpeas seed has been found in case of CQDs derived from biological waste (CQD5 > CQD6 > CQD4) followed by plastic waste derived CQDs (CQD1 > CQD2 > CQD3) in both the case of dry and sprouted seeds. Conversely, Barley seeds have displayed maximum germination ability in presence of CQD5 followed by CQD4 > CQD6 > CQD2 > CQD3 > CQD1 after 3rd and 7th day of exposure for dry and sprouted seeds. In case of Mung beans seeds, CQD5 shown the maximum root length followed by CQD4 > CQD6 > CQD3 > CQD2 > CQD1. For Wheat seeds, the maximum average root length has been observed for CQD6 followed by CQD5 > CQD4 > CQD3 > CQD2 > CQD1. In addition, the maximum optimal concentration has been found to be 3000 mg/L during the emergence of rootlets for all the chosen particles (Fig. 5).

The significant increment in the respective value of root length and germination ability has further supported the growth of nuclei in plants. However, the average root length has shown the slight decrement in the value with enhancing the concentration of CQDs to 5000 mg/L. During seed germination process, the primary roots emerged out and protected by root-tip. However, the primary shoots have been protected by coleoptile (covering of shoot tip) in seeds. Therefore, the emergence of coleoptiles length has also been investigated in presence of CQDs along with the rootlets. It has been observed that the normal light exposure has played an inhibitory effect over the growth of coleoptile length. However, the rootlets have shown the linear increment in growth in presence of normal light. In addition, the coleoptile length of chosen seeds has further depended upon the energy stored in the seed. The presence of CQDs has affected the length of coleoptiles as a function of time of exposure in presence of nanoparticles. The coleoptiles length has found to be linearly increased after 3 days of exposure in the presence of CQDs as a function of concentration changes from 250 mg/L to 3000 mg/L (Fig. S4). The average increment of coleoptile length has marginally enhanced on 7th day as compared to 3rd day. On comparing the chosen seeds, it has been found that the Chickpeas and Wheat have exhibited maximum length of coleoptiles as compared to Barley and Mung beans. Moreover, biomass derived CQDs has shown maximum value of average root length and coleoptiles in all seeds. For instance, CQD5 has shown maximum emergence of coleoptiles length followed by CQD6 > CQD4 > CQD1 > CQD2 > CQD3. In addition, 3000 mg/L concentration is considered as the most appropriate concentration for the maximum growth of coleoptiles length. From here, it can be considered that biomass derived C-dots have better affinity towards plant germination. Therefore, they can be further utilized in agricultural field application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of seed germination, germination index and percentage inhibition growth
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity assessment on seed germination in presence of prepared CQDs has not only shown the benefits of proposed system but also enhanced the environment related advancement of CQDs in agricultural field. It can be inferred that the biocompatible and non-toxic behaviour of CQDs does not only depends upon their nature and concentration but also depends upon the type and quality of utilized seeds. The value of germinating ability was found to be same (100%) in all cases of CQDs with respect to the concentration after 3 and 7 days of exposure (Table S3). The seeds of Chickpeas and Mung beans have reflected the maximum adsorption capacity due to their efflorescing morphology. However, it was found to be minimum in case of Barley. However, in case of Wheat seeds, all the kernels are plasmolyzed but the frequency of plasmolysis was found to be slightly lower in case of higher concentration (5000 mg/L) of chosen CQDs. This has further suggested the slight decrement in the germinating abilities of Wheat at higher concentration, but the obtained values were much higher as compared to the control system. The exposure time has also displayed linear enhancement in the root length of all seeds increases as a function of concentrations of CQDs.

The positive potential and augmented productivity of plants under optimal conditions has further been investigated by observing the values of germination index of seedlings in presence of CQDs. The germination index values have displayed the linear increment on varying the concentrations of CQDs (Fig. S5). It has further been noticed that the chosen CQDs have no ability to block the aquaporins (transmembrane of water channels) in seeds which further controlled and enhanced the water permeability in seeds. Therefore, the chosen CQDs have played a fundamental role in the germination rate of plants. For instance, CQD5 and CQD6 have reflected maximum value ~122% of germination index after 3rd days of exposure in case of Chickpeas and Barley followed by CQD4 > CQD3 > CQD2 > CQD1. For Mung beans, CQD5 has displayed the germination index value to 120% followed by CQD6 > CQD4 > CQD3 > CQD1 > CQD2. For Wheat, the maximum value of 117% was obtained for CQD6 followed by CQD4 > CQD5 > CQD3 > CQD2 > CQD1. However, the value of GI values has followed the similar pattern after 7th day of exposure in presence of CQDs. The inhibition rate of germination has been calculated for all the chosen seeds in presence of different types of prepared CQDs (Tables S4 and S5). From the calculated values, it was observed that value of PIG was found negligible in all the cases which suggest the non-toxic and biocompatible behaviour of developed CQDs in presence of utilized four seeds. The value of PIG decreases as the concentration of CQDs increased from 250 mg/L to 5000 mg/L which further suggested the supporting nature of CQDs towards the growth of plants. From the obtained results, it can be concluded that the negligible value of PIG suggests the maximum germination ability and germination index values which were exactly similar to the above obtained results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Vigor Index I and Seed Vigor II in presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The characteristic properties of CQDs have played an important role on the anti-proliferative activity of seeds especially on the Vigor Index values of chosen seeds. The value of Vigor index I mainly affects germination rate and average length of seedlings. As, the germination rate has been found to be 100% in each case, therefore the value of average root length has affected the Vigor index in chosen seeds (Fig. 6 ). The value of average root length was found maximum at 3000 mg/L concentration level for each seed in presence of CQDs.

From here, it can be inferred that 3000 mg/L concentration of each CQDs will provide maximum value of Vigor Index II. In case of Chickpeas, CQD5 and CQD6 have displayed maximum Vigor index values as compared to other developed particles. In case of Barley, CQD6 has reflected maximum value of Vigor index of 452 as followed by CQD5 > CQD4 > CQD3 > CQD1 > CQD2. For Mung beans CQD5 has shown maximum value of Vigor index of 502 as followed by CQD6 > CQD4 > CQD3 > CQD1 > CQD2. For Wheat CQD6 has reflected maximum value of Vigor index of 502 followed by CQD5 > CQD4 > CQD3 > CQD2 > CQD1. The obtained results have further suggested that biomass derived CQDs have maximum Vigor Index values as compared to the plastic derived CQDs. Additionally, the enhancement in biocompatibility has been further found to be more in case of CQDs, which are directly sprouted in different concentration of CQDs as compared to dry seeds. Further, the value of Seed Vigor II was calculated for the optimized system (Fig. S6).

The value of Seed Vigor II index majorly depends upon two factors i.e., germination ability and weight of dry seedlings. As, the value of germination ability found to be 100% in all the cases, it can be inferred that value of Seed Vigor II only affected by the weight of dry seedlings (mg). During germination study, the average weight of all seeds taken which slightly vary in each case (±5%). More the weight of dry seedlings more will be the value of Seed Vigor index. The value of seed Vigor was found similar in all the cases due to the similar weight of seedlings. From the obtained seeds, it can be concluded that Vigor Index I and Seed Vigor II was found to be maximum in 3000 mg/L concentration for each type of C-dots. From the above-mentioned activities, it can be concluded that Chickpeas, Barley, Mung beans and Wheat has shown germination in presence of both the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chlorophyll content and water uptake activity of Mung beans seeds in presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The chlorophyll content is an important parameter during plant growth which is indirectly related to the photosynthetic rate, total quantity of light harvesting complex and electron transport components. CQDs have the tendency to penetrate into the leaves of different plant and augment the nature of thylakoid sheaths which in turn increases the amount of chlorophyll content and leads to proper and mature growth of plants. In the present work, the amount of chlorophyll content was calculated only for Mung beans seeds after 7th days of normal light exposure (Fig. 7 ). The emergence of leaves was only observed in Mung beans seeds, as compared to Chickpeas, Barley and Wheat seeds. Therefore, Mung beans seeds have only been used for the analysis of chlorophyll content in case of both dry and sprouted CQDs (Fig. 7). On interpreting the data, it has been found that the chlorophyll content was maximum in case of 3000 mg/L concentration for each type of chosen CQDs in comparison to control system which further suggest the maximum photosynthesis activity of plants due to the presence of maximum number of adsorption sites on the surface of CQDs. However, at very high and lower concentration level (250 and 5000 mg/L), the amount of chlorophyll content was slightly lower as compared to 3000 mg/L concentration level but higher as compared to the control system which further suggested the better activity of developed system at all the concentration level. The decrement in the chlorophyll content has further been related to the growth and metabolism process of seed which alters the germination rate and photosynthesis process in Mung beans. For dry seeds, the order of chlorophyll content is CQD4 > CQD6 > CQD5 > CQD3 > CQD2 > CQD1. However, for the sprouted seeds, the maximum chlorophyll content was found in CQD4 followed by CQD6 > CQD5 > CQD1 > CQD3 > CQD2.

Further on interpreting the water uptake activity (moisture content) of Mung beans, it has been suggested the influencing behaviour of chosen CQDs on the cellular metabolism of sprouted seeds. The moisture content was measured by thermogravimetric analysis (weight loss process) in temperature range of 20 to 250 °C for dried seeds. The moisture level of seeds dipped in different concentration of CQDs has also been compared (Fig. 7c). From the analysis, the moisture content was found to be 21% in case of control (dry seeds) whereas 25% was observed in case of water exposed Mung beans seeds. The amount of moisture content has further been increased linearly with concentration of CQDs. The maximum moisture content has been reported in case of 3000 mg/L concentration for each type of CQDS due to their maximum germinating ability followed by 5000 mg/L > 1000 mg/L > 500 mg/L > 250 mg/L > water absorbed seeds > dry seeds. From here, it can be concluded that developed CQDs have accelerated the water uptake activity of sprouted Mung beans seeds.

An informative and collective discussion has been drawn for the utilized seed in presence of different kinds of CQDs. The direct exposure of edible seeds to the aqueous solution of CQDs has provided the better growth results as compared to control system. The existence of CQDs has further supported the water permeability in the seeds and played a fundamental role in the germination rate of chosen seeds. This has mainly been explained due to the larger surface area and excellent physicochemical and absorption properties of chosen CQDs. Additionally, the acquaintances of plant in presence of CQDs have decreased the production of reactive oxygen species which in turn improved their germinating and proliferative behaviour. It is very important to explore the influence of CQDs on different species and parts of plants because it is still very uncertain and inconsistent in literature whether they are toxic in nature or they enhance abiotic stress in plants. From the current study, it was observed that up to 3000 mg/L concentration of CQDs have not produced any kind of blockage in the aquaporins (trans-membrane of water channels) of seeds. However, the higher concentration slightly affects the germination process. The presence of higher concentration of CQDs has the ability to dehydrate the kernels and produced abnormalities in their morphology which in turn affects the germination process of seeds. The reason of slight decrement of germination index in case of 5000 mg/L concentration might be due to the blockage of pores in presence of excessive amount of CQDs which further affected the water uptake behaviour in seeds and influenced their germination activities. The reason behind the declining ability of germination has been explained due to the blockage of the seed pores in presence of excessive amount of CQDs. This blockage has further affected the water uptake activity of chosen seed in presence of CQDs. In addition, the frequency of seed plasmolysis has also shown the decrement in presence of higher concentration (5000 mg/L) of CQDs. Simultaneously, the different functional groups present over the surface of CQDs have the ability to influence the seed germination activities in chosen seeds. The bio-mass derived CQDs have shown higher rate of germination as compared to the plastic derived C-dots. Further, the moisture level of Mung beans seeds has increased remarkably when treated with different concentration of CQDs which in-turn indicates the enhancement in water uptake activity of seeds. The presence of CQDs has the ability to improve the permeability rate in Mung beans seeds which leads to augmentation in water absorption process.

In the current work, the influential behaviour of plastic waste and biomass waste derived CQDs have been tested on the germination ability of four different types of seed. A simple, single step and eco-friendly approach has been utilized for the production of six different types of CQDs after avoiding the use of perilous precursors and solvents. The root length, germination rate and Vigor index of seed has been compared as a function of dry and sprouted seeds after the exposure time of 3rd and 7th day for different concentrations of CQDS. The value of different parameters was found maximum after 7 days of exposure for all types of dry seed due to their direct contact with CQDs. All the chosen CQDs have displayed dose dependent response behaviour on the biological activity of seeds. The maximum germination for different types of seed was observed at 3000 mg/L concentration level of CQDs. The value of percentage inhibition growth was found to be negligible for each type of seeds at every concentration level. Moreover, C-dots have also affected the essential marker such as chlorophyll content of Mung beans seed and significantly augmented after 7 days' exposure for dry seeds as compared to sprouted seeds. The biomass derived CQDs have possessed more biocompatibility as compared to the plastic derived CQDS. The obtained findings have suggested that the developed CQDs have possessed outstanding applications in agricultural field. The information's achieved herein will allow the usage of C-dots as a capable nano-priming agent for natural germination of seeds and enhanced their agronomical paradigm.

Savita Chaudhary; Pooja Chauhan and Manisha Kumari have collected the literature information. Pooja Chauhan and Manisha Kumari performed all the experimental work in details. Pooja Chauhan and Manisha Kumari collected all the data, information regarding the same paper and compiled in the form of paper. G.R. Chaudhary, Ahmad Umar, S. Baskoutas and Savita Chaudhary read and corrected the manuscript.
